coredata.dc:title: Glucose detection by gold modified carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots-based surface plasmon resonance
coredata.dc:creator: Rosddi, Najwa Norimanina Muhammad
Fen, Yap Wing
Omar, Nur Alia Sheh
Anas, Nur Ain Asyiqin
Hashim, Hazwani Suhaila
Ramdzan, Nur Syahira Md
Fauzi, Nurul Illya Mohd
Anuar, Muhammad Fahmi
Daniyal, Wan Mohd Ebtisyam Mustaqim Mohd
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a graphene quantum dots composite thin film-based surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensor was developed and experimentally demonstrated for glucose detection. The proposed work incorporates the modification of SPR gold film with carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots-nanocrystalline cellulose (CGQDs-NCC) to improve the detection sensitivity of glucose. The sensing performances of gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film as a function of the sensitivity and binding affinity were analysed. The experimental results show that the linear response of the gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film were 13.296° μM−1, 0.13983° μM−1 and 0.00753° μM−1 for the 0 μM to 0.03 μM, 0.03 μM to 0.5 μM and 0.5 μM–10 μM of glucose concentrations, respectively. The binding affinity has been analyzed using Langmuir and Sips model where it revealed that gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film has higher affinity constant as compared to unmodified gold film. All in all, this paper gives the idea that the introduction of gold-modified CGQDs-NCC thin film in SPR has good potential for detecting glucose solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Nanocrystalline cellulose
Thin film
Surface plasmon resonance
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CGQDs-NCC thin film combined with SPR
SPR reflectance curve of gold thin film
SPR reflectance curve of gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film
Comparison in sensitivity and binding affinity analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded through the Fundamental Research Grant Scheme (FRGS) (FRGS/1/2019/STG02/UPM/02/1) and Putra Grant Universiti Putra Malaysia by Ministry of Education Malaysia .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of SPR set-up and glucose detection under Krestchmann configuration.
SPR reflectance curve of gold layer thin film contacted with DW.
SPR reflectance curves of gold layer contacted with various concentrations of glucose solution ranged from 0.005 μM to 10.0 μM.
SPR curve of gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film contacted with DW.
SPR reflectance curves of gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film contacted with various concentration of glucose solution ranged from (a) 0.0-0.1 μM and (b) 0.1-10.0 μM.
The shift of resonance angle for gold thin film and gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film.
The binding affinity based on (a) Langmuir (b) Sips isotherm model for various concentration of glucose solution contacted with gold thin film and gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film.
The shift of resonance angle for gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film contacted with various concentrations of glucose solution.
The comparison of detection limit and sensitivity for glucose detection using surface plasmon resonance sensors.

row.entry: Glucose oxidase@silica mesocellular foams + SiO2 nanoparticles (GOD@SiMCFs + SiNPs)

head.ce:title: Glucose detection by gold modified carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots-based surface plasmon resonance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400, UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400, UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Technology, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400, UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Advanced Technology, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400, UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400, UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a graphene quantum dots composite thin film-based surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensor was developed and experimentally demonstrated for glucose detection. The proposed work incorporates the modification of SPR gold film with carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots-nanocrystalline cellulose (CGQDs-NCC) to improve the detection sensitivity of glucose. The sensing performances of gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film as a function of the sensitivity and binding affinity were analysed. The experimental results show that the linear response of the gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film were 13.296° μM−1, 0.13983° μM−1 and 0.00753° μM−1 for the 0 μM to 0.03 μM, 0.03 μM to 0.5 μM and 0.5 μM–10 μM of glucose concentrations, respectively. The binding affinity has been analyzed using Langmuir and Sips model where it revealed that gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film has higher affinity constant as compared to unmodified gold film. All in all, this paper gives the idea that the introduction of gold-modified CGQDs-NCC thin film in SPR has good potential for detecting glucose solution.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes is a common carbohydrate metabolic disease associated with many complications caused by deficiency of insulin production associated with type 1 diabetes, or the inability of cells to response to insulin leading to type 2 diabetes [1–3]. Monitoring the abnormal body blood glucose level is vital for indicators of human health. World Health Organization estimated over 500 million people will be diagnosed with diabetes in 2030 if it is not controlled and untreated [4,5]. The precise and fast detection of glucose level in blood is of great significance, henceforth the routine testing of physiological glucose levels is crucial to prevent any diabetic emergencies and reduce associated complications. Due to this reason, a number of sensors incorporated with nanomaterials have been reported to measure glucose levels as well as to simplify the testing process and enhance its sensitivity [6–11].

Surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensor is observed as a sensitive optical method for interfacial and surface studies and shows great unrealized monitoring the chemical and biological species via the basic theory of SPR detection [12–16]. Despite its high sensitivity, SPR sensor has a limitation in detecting low concentration of glucose molecules, as it measures the change in refractive index on the surface of gold film [17]. Coating a gold film with a promising semiconductor nanomaterial, graphene quantum dots will help in increasing the refractive index of the dielectric sensing media, which consequently increases the absorption of glucose [18–20].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have become attractive in various applications, particularly for diagnostics, drug delivery, electronic displays, and other optoelectronic devices due to its excellent electrical conductivity, mechanical properties, biocompatibility, and transparency [21–31]. Nevertheless, the practical applications of GQDs in sensor are limited in providing more active area in specific contact with analyte. Functionalizing GQDs with carboxylic acid (−COOH) groups can increases specific surface area for analyte absorption [32]. Elucidating the effect of CGQDs with NCC is valuable for selective detection of glucose. NCC has received tremendous attention in a myriad of fields owing to its extraordinary strength, large surface area, flexible surface chemistry, biocompatible, and eco-friendly [33–36]. Hence, the composite of CGQDs-NCC is no doubt becoming a great glucose sensor layer modified the surface of gold film.

In this paper, the application of CGQDs-NCC in glucose sensor is demonstrated using SPR sensor. The sensing performances of the CGQDs-NCC thin film based SPR sensor, i.e., SPR response, linear sensitivity, and binding affinity are discussed.

Hexadecyltrimethyl ammonim bromide (CTA) and NCC were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). CGQDs solution and glucose were purchased from ACS Material, America and R&M Marketing, Essex, U.A, respectively.

Glucose with a weight of 9.91 mg was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water (DW) to producing glucose solution with a concentration of 10 μM. Next, dilution formula M1V1=M2V2 was used for the dilution of the 10 μM of glucose solution with DW for the preparation of glucose solution with concentrations of 0.005 μM, 0.01 μM, 0.03 μM, 0.05 μM, 0.1 μM, 0.3 μM, 0.5 μM, 1.0 μM, 3.0 μM and 5.0 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CGQDs-NCC thin film combined with SPR
ce:section.ce:para: 1 g of NCC was diluted in 100 mL DW to prepare NCC solution. In this study, NCC was modified with CTA by using following preparation: CTA solution was firstly prepared by the dilution of 0.2 g of CTA in 20 mL of DW before NCC solution was dropped into it while heat stirring for 24 h. Next, the modified NCC was centrifuged at 3000 rev/min for 15 min and was further diluted by 8 mL of DW. Afterwards, 0.5 M of CGQDs was mixed with modified NCC under volume ratio of 1:1 followed by a slow stirring for 5 min.

To prepare gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film, approximately of 1000 μL of composite solution was pipetted on a gold coated glass slip (24 mm × 24 mm × 0.1 mm) followed by the spinning process at 3000 rev/min for 30 s. Afterwards, the gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film was evaporated onto prism surface using index matching liquid. The prism then was placed on an optical stage driven by stepper motor with a resolution of 0.001° (Newport MM 3000) to carry out the SPR measurement. First and foremost, 1 mL of DW was injected into the o-ring for reference signal, followed by injecting the glucose solution with various concentrations ranging of 0.005 μM to 10.0 μM one after another. The detection of glucose solution was based on the interaction of p-polarized light with plasmons on the gold surface and excited them under Kretschmann configuration as shown in Fig. 1 [37]. At the prism-gold film interface, an evanescent field has been generated, representing the formation of an evanescent wave which will penetrate to the lower refractive index medium at a depth of about 150 nm [38,39]. This evanescent wave is called as the sensing component as it has high sensitivity to the any changes in the refractive index of the dielectric. Changes in refractive index will influence the coupling of the evanescent wave with the surface plasmon, which accounted for the shift of the SPR reflectance curve against incident angle [40,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: SPR reflectance curve of gold thin film
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the SPR reflectance curve of gold layer thin film contacted with DW. The DW was left in 10 min. in cell for detection purpose. The resonance angle obtained was 54.02°. Next, the detection was followed by glucose solution with various concentrations, starting from 0.005 μM to 10.0 μM. From Fig. 3 , a similarity in the resonance angle of all glucose concentrations and DW can be observed. There were no changes in resonance angle values for the glucose solution contacted with a bare gold thin film as there is no change in refractive index of gold surface. This is because of the limitation of the gold thin film based SPR sensor in measuring similar refractive index of DW and low concentration of glucose solution [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: SPR reflectance curve of gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film
ce:section.ce:para: The SPR experiment was continued by modifying a gold surface with CGQDs-NCC layer. First and foremost, the SPR reflectance curve of gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film contacted with DW is shown in Fig. 4 . The resonance angle obtained was 54.39°, slightly higher than previous resonance angle for gold layer thin film only. The increase in resonance angle is due to the change in the refractive index adjacent to the metal surface caused by deposition of CGQDs-NCC layer, which affects the resonance condition [43].

Next, glucose solution with various concentrations, starting from 0.005 μM to 10.0 μM was injected, contacting the gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film surface. As one can see in Fig. 5 , the resonance angle increases towards a higher resonance angle as the concentration of glucose solution increases [44,45]. The significant shift in resonance angle among glucose concentration was calculated and tabulated in Table 1 . From table, the shift in resonance angle increased as the refractive index at the sensing region increased due to the increase in glucose concentration. The change in refractive index of the sensing region resulted from the successful binding of glucose molecules to the surface of gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film [46]. All in all, it can be noted that the gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film has a significant change in resonance angle when detecting the lowest concentration of glucose at 0.005 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison in sensitivity and binding affinity analysis
ce:section.ce:para: A linear regression analysis method is used to measure the correlation of SPR response and the concentration of glucose solution in determining the sensitivity of the sensor [47]. The method was done using linear fit tool in Origin 9 software. A linear regression of y = mx + c will be obtained, where m is the slope that can be defined as the sensitivity of the SPR sensor and c is the vertical intercept. Fig. 6 shows the experimental data and the linear fit of gold thin film and gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film. From linear regression analysis of gold thin film, the slope of the line was 0° μM−1 as there were no changes in the refractive index of the gold surface when detecting all concentrations of glucose solution. While for linear regression analysis of gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film, the slope line was divided to three part, revealed the sensitivity of 13.296° μM−1, 0.13983° μM−1 and 0.00753° μM−1 for the ranges of concentrations of 0 to 0.03 μM, 0.03 to 0.5 μM, and 0.5–10 μM. Thus, this SPR sensor based on gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film is concluded to be able to detect glucose down to 0.005 μM and gave a sensitivity of 13.296° μM−1. Finally, the performance of the proposed sensor is compared with other recent works on glucose detection using SPR sensor as shown in Table 2 [48–52]. It shows that the proposed sensor has highest sensitivity and lowest detection limit, indicating the gold/CGQDs-NCC layer successfully enhances the performance of SPR sensor.

The non-linear fitting based on Langmuir isotherm model was used to derive the binding affinity of SPR sensor based gold thin film and gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film towards glucose molecules (Fig. 7 a). The Langmuir isotherm model equation is as follows [53]:
(1)
Δ θ =
Δ θ
m a x
C
1 K
+ C
where Δ θ is the shift of resonance angle, C is the concentration of glucose solution, and K is the binding affinity constant. The affinity constant of glucose to gold thin film was 0.9919 μM−1 whereas the affinity constant of glucose to gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film was 123.15 μM−1. It can be seen that the gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film has higher affinity constant than gold thin film. The higher binding affinity constant, the stronger interaction of glucose molecules towards sensor surface [54]. Despite high affinity constant, its correlation coefficient, R2 was poor with the value of 0.9431, indicates that the model does not fit the data well. Therefore, in this study, the experimental data for gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film was fitted to Sips isotherm model equation:
(2)
Δ θ =
Δ θ
m a x
( K s C )
n
1 +
( K s C )
n
where Ks is the Sips affinity constant, C is the concentration of glucose solution, and n is heterogeneity index of the system, which can be varied from 0 to 1 [55]. From Fig. 7(b), the experimental was well fitted to Sips model with R2 of 0.9636 compared to the Langmuir model and the n value obtained was 0.39, indicating a heterogenous surface.

The SPR sensor with gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film has been fabricated and experimentally demonstrated for glucose detection. As compared to the unmodified gold thin film, the SPR sensor with gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film exhibited good refractive index changes when interact with glucose solution. Particularly, the proposed sensor has higher sensitivity of 13.296° μM−1 with detectable glucose concentration of 0.005 μM. Moreover, the binding affinity of glucose and gold/CGQDs-NCC thin film has a better fit using the Sips model (R2 = 0.9636) than the Langmuir model. This finding validates that this novel CGQDs-NCC thin film has good potential as a sensing surface for glucose using SPR.

Conceptualization, methodology, writing—original draft preparation, N.N.M.R.; validation, supervision, writing—review and editing, funding acquisition, Y.W.F; formal analysis, writing—review and editing, N.A.S.O., N.A.A.A, N.S.M.R.; visualization, H.S.H., N.I.M.F, M.F.A, and W.M.E.M.M.D. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.
This research was funded through the Fundamental Research Grant Scheme (FRGS) (FRGS/1/2019/STG02/UPM/02/1) and Putra Grant Universiti Putra Malaysia by Ministry of Education Malaysia.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The laboratory facilities provided by the Institute of Advanced Technology, Department of Physics, and Department of Chemistry, Universiti Putra Malaysia are acknowledged.
